preamble: The Voice Loss of the initial velar stop [g] to [k] is a foundational mechanism of Tonogenesis in Southeast Asia. This devoicing triggered a Register Split, where the originally voiced initial [g] resulted in a lower Pitch Contour (the Yáng or low register), phonemically distinct from the higher pitch associated with original [k].
phoneticEffects: Voice Loss, Devoicing, Register Split, Tonogenesis

examples.note: Initial voicing loss in velar consonants contributed to the creation of the low-tone register (huyền, nặng, ngã) in Vietnamese.
Early Middle Chinese [g] (Quánzhuó) devoiced, causing the split of traditional tone categories into high (Yīn) and low (Yáng) registers.
In Proto-Tai, initial *g is reconstructed as the source for the low-register tonal contours observed in modern Thai and related languages.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Pittayaporn, P. (2009). The Phonology of Proto-Tai.
